title: Oil Isn't Dead — John Love on Why Energy Markets Are Defying the Headlines

segments.text: Growing a successful business has never been easy,
but whether you're navigating a complex industry or trying to reinvent one,
our breadth of banking experts are here to collaborate on market-proven solutions.
At KeyBank, we're built to grow with your changing needs,
because we're not just here for the business you are now.
We're here for the business you'll become.
Re-imagine business as usual.
KeyBank opens doors.

LinkedIn is pretty amazing at helping you grow your small business.
We cannot stop your new clients from emailing you at 3AM.
We can help you sell, market, and hire in one place.
We cannot help you be in three places at once.
And while we can't help you organize your calendar,
LinkedIn can help you land more clients so you have a calendar to organize.
Grow your small business on LinkedIn.
Learn more at linkedin.com slash small business.
Well, live. This is value after hours. I'm Tobias Carlyle.
I'll join as always by my co-host Jake Taylor.
Our special guest today is John Love.
He's the president and CEO of UCF Investments.
There are commodity energy ETF firm.
John's going to give us some views on where copper and oil are going.
How are you, John? Welcome to the show.
Thanks for having me. I'm doing good. Thank you.
Tell us a little bit about the phone.
Sure. Well, UCF Investments, the acronym is short for United States commodity funds.
We've been around for 20 years. We launched our first ETF in 2006,
April 2006, so just celebrated a 20th anniversary.
We were the first oil ETF, the third commodity ETF to exist,
and had a lot of success with that. A lot of people are familiar with the ticker,
USO. That's us more so than the firm, but we shortened United States commodity funds to
USCF Investments because we branched out a little bit in the ensuing years.
But our product lineup is primarily commodity ETFs.
So after oil, we did natural gas. We did kind of a contango mitigating fund.
We've done copper, and we've also branched into broad commodities,
where we think we have a pretty good strategy that's done really good this decade so far.
It's actually beaten the S&P and NASDAQ 100 over the last five years.
So pretty proud of that. So yeah, we just keep trying to innovate in the space,
and that's us in a nutshell.
Congrats. I think copper is particularly interesting,
potentially because it's used in AI. I've been telling a story that the world needs six more
S&D and we don't have eight more S&D and we don't have eight more S&D.
Tell us a little bit about what's happening in copper.
Yeah, I think you nailed it. The challenge right now is the short term versus the long term.
Like everybody knows, I don't know if everybody knows. But over the next five years,
I think it's a pretty established fact that unless there's an interruption to this AI
story, there is not enough, in fact, even before AI, there's not enough copper for all of the things
that we're trying to do. Just standard manufacturing and growth, but then you add EVs,
you let electrification across the board, and then the AI story data centers coming into it.
There's just not enough copper. It takes a long time to bring a new supply online that's starting
to happen, but it's a very long process. People are saying 10 years. It's probably more 15,
depending on your jurisdiction. It's probably the rest of your life. I mean, it's just
it's a challenging thing to do. In the short term, it's a little harder to say. If you look like it
analysts right now from Goldman to JP Morgan, I think that's the people on both ends of the
spectrum. Goldman is saying there's a surplus of copper. JP Morgan is saying there's a deficit
right now. It's pretty hard to nail down, where are we at the moment? What's going to happen?
One thing that has been ahead when has been the Iran War, just because if that slows the global
economy, copper still is tied to that. On the short term, what's going on could affect things.
It's affected China's manufacturing. PMI, the reading is slightly bearish.
They've slowed down here and there, but they also have committed to a tremendous amount of
new grid capacity, which requires a vast amount of copper. Just across the board, I think the
copper story is bullish. In the short term, we always have to be careful. It's hard to say,
when might UCA downturn in the global economy, how might that affect things and will the long-term
term supply deficit override that anyway? That's definite possibility.
For all the things you do in places you go, let Uber take you there, because you had just one more
three rounds ago, because the 5am flight seemed smart at the time, because check engine lights
appear at the worst times. Because parking downtown is a nightmare. Whatever your reason,
there are drivers in your area. Uber, request a ride today and get your first ride free with promo
code first free Uber 2026. Wait time varies, terms and conditions apply, see after details.
Not totally. What do you like about it? You love the high-quality ingredients
and the delicious flavors, of course. Yeah, that makes a ton of sense. Listen to Percy,
guys. Visit PerfectBeastro.com to try it for your cat.
When you think about copper's role as Dr. Copper, is it still the doctor or is it less so, I think?
Yeah, I think, yes, it used to be very predictive. Now, because of the deficit,
there's more of its own fundamental story that it could actually, we could be in a situation
that kind of think we are where there's some real headwinds in the global economy.
And yet, Copper over the last couple of years has done quite well, and it sort of keeps
buff, I mean, obviously stocks have done quite well as well, but I think if you look at some
of the things that tend to lead to downturns, Copper isn't in that same place. So, I think there's
certainly Dr. Copper still has his white coat in the closet somewhere, but I don't know if it's
as much as it used to be. I just want to give one of the metrics that I like to track is Copper
and Gold together just to take out the US dollar influence, and obviously Gold had a very good run
until the last few months, and Copper's had a good run alongside it, which to me might be a
little bit counterintuitive because Gold tends to be more of a asset that runs into stress, whereas
Copper seems to be more predictive of a stronger economy, and they're both running together
and if you look at that Copper Gold ratio, it's actually troughed recently, and it's sort of
in a place where you would see it at the bottom of pretty substantial processions and stock market
drawdowns. So, to me, it looks like in it's like a 2016, 2009, 2000 kind of bottom versus Gold,
but if you look at the stock market, you wouldn't have known that that was happening,
which I find kind of an interesting thing. Do you have any view on what might be causing that,
or have you observed anything like that? Yeah, I think, well, you know, just like Copper,
maybe not having that, you know, as much of a role in prediction. I think Gold is at least this
year is, you know, somewhat confounding as well, because everybody thinks Risk asset,
and then, you know, it hits an all-time high in January, and then you have the Ironmore breakout
and you'd expect, well, that's going to drive it up even more, and you still have all the
tailwinds for Gold that we had before the Ironmore broke out. And yet, it did quite poorly over the
last, I mean, I guess not terrible, but not as we would expect over the last three months,
and then, you know, with the announcement of the, the Iran deal, you know, it's back up,
everything it's moving back up. So, I think both of them have been, you know, a little confounding,
and so, you know, you know, as far as that metric, I tend to look at the Gold Silver Ratio, that's
when we get asked about more, but that is a really good one to look at is the Gold Copper,
and I'll have to take a look at that more, but I did, to be honest, I haven't,
I haven't looked at it, but yeah, they've both been performing, which is definitely counterintuitive,
because usually Gold's the risk, Risk Off asset, and Copper's the Risk On asset, and now they seem
to have, you know, they seem to be playing, you know, both Risk On and Risk Off in somewhat
unpredictable ways. Gold, obviously, there is a, you know, there is some, you know, reason for
for what's happened, but it still has all those tailwinds. I mean, Central Bank buying has not dropped
off at all. In fact, it was a record, I think, in, or the highest level, it had been Q1 versus the
previous five years, and none of the G20 economies have stopped buying gold, so that's still going on.
I think you still have, with the, you know, potential for, I don't know if we're going to have
interest rate, be able to do interest rate cuts or not. I'm kind of a inflation, worried about
inflation myself, but it certainly changes the probability and the odds, if we don't have the
war going on. So, so we'll see, but yeah, it's interesting for, for both those metals that they're,
you know, they're behaving in different ways relative to the past, and it's not just just
black and white cut and dry as it has been. You raised it, and I find it, it's another interesting
ratio that I tracked to silver gold when, when gold was very, very strong, it sort of took silver
a little while to catch fire, but when silver catches fire, it really does run hard against gold,
and it went parabolic, and when it, I posted a chart on Twitter when it went parabolic, saying this
is typically closer to the end of the, of the run of these monetary metals, when they do that,
and it's seem to be close to the breakdown, but what is the, what does the gold silver ratio
tell you? Yeah, I mean, I mean, I think kind of the textbook thing is, you know, when it gets low,
that means silver's doing well relative to gold, and where it is right now, is gold's back
on top. So, you know, when I first discovered it a long time ago, I thought, oh, this is brilliant,
you know, if it's, you know, if gold's running at a huge premium to silver, then just invest in silver,
but of course you can sit there for years, and you know, but when it happens, just like you said,
it's high-bated to gold, so when it does move, it tends to move a lot and be a very rewarding trade,
but you know, then you, you annualize that over how many years you were waiting for it to pop,
and it might not quite be as, as exciting. So, I do think it's worth looking at, you know,
if you, you know, run-moving averages on it, or you have a system that can kind of, you know,
tell you when it's time, time to get in, and like you said, when it goes parabolic,
this definitely an interesting trade, but it works both ways. You've got that high beta to work with.
I saw that, one of the interesting charts that I like to track is the 10-year,
or sorry, the two-year treasury as a proxy for what the Fed might do with rates, so I think that
that's not an uncommon thing to do, but since the Iran War, that the two-year has run up very
substantially over the Fed funds rate, where it's been a bit of a discount for a while, which to your
point seems to make the likelihood of rate cuts low. If anything, it looks like we might be seeing
interest rate rises. Do you have any view there? Yeah, I mean, I think interest rate
steadiness or rises are the logical, rational thing right now.
Politics being what they are, you know, there is possibility that we get rate cuts anyway,
which would obviously, you know, be beneficial for not just gold, but many assets, but yeah,
I do think the market is telling the government, and you certainly have seen the chatter calm down
about, you know, we have a new Fed share who's supposed to be, you know, more dovish. Yeah,
yeah, then Powell, and yet there hasn't been any chatter about cutting rates, and I think a lot of
that is, you know, everybody knows when the bond market is signaling, okay, this is getting out of
control rates are going up. People are demanding that higher premium for not the two-year,
but just longer as well. You know, I think you have to take that into account. I mean,
that can be absolutely disastrous if we don't, and we have, you look at the deficit, and that
is just the most frustrating thing to me in the world is that you have 30 years, almost,
in 25 years of, you know, running up, running up deficits when we actually had a surplus,
and now the debt is what it is, and I think I saw a headline yesterday, we went from a trillion-dollar
debt to a trillion-dollar deficit. It's just, I'm sorry, trillion dollars in interest payments. I mean,
it's just unbelievable. So I think the bond market is reacting to that. You need some kind of
catalyst, explosive growth, AI saving us from ourselves, but we've got to have something. Otherwise,
I am worried about where interest rates go. Well, I can't believe it's taken us this long to get
there, but let's talk oil. Is it the most interesting market in the world of the world? What's happening
in oil? Oh, not much. It's been quite. Yeah, it's been, I would say, the most exciting three months,
you know, for bad reasons. Obviously, wars is never, you know, something you want to see, but it's
been such a, such a back and forth, and, you know, just bizarre. You know, when it first broke out,
obviously, that was the, you know, the most exciting point now, you know, since April, we've
been in a different place. I think there's some skepticism about what happens now, but, you know,
where we are versus where we were. I'd say, you know, entering the war period, I think the
White House and some people in the market are a little skeptical now of higher prices.
You know, there's still risk out there, that's what I'm trying to say. And I think there's a lot
of skepticism about that risk because some of the, the most bearish calls, in fact, bearish calls
that were pretty common, you know, we're going to have $150 oil, we're going to have $200 oil.
At first, it was the end of March. And then it's like, okay, we did the coordinated SPR release
in the G7. And so now we have totally in April. And, you know, that got pushed out. And I think
everybody's kind of like, well, did the industry cry wolf? It might have been this materialized.
And it's really because you know, not just the SPR release, but there are a number of things
that I think people just, you know, at the time couldn't foresee, you know, we, I think one thing
that we always discount is how creative industry can be in the face of crisis. And one of the things
we've seen is China had built up their reserves substantially over the last the previous three
years. And they were able to rely on those, draw those down. That's not going to last forever.
If this doesn't get resolved, I mean, this doesn't truly get resolved as everybody's expecting
right now. But that had a big effect on the price of oil and kept it, kept it down.
Just before you move on, for that, do you have any view on what caused that? Was that China accommodating
the U.S. by throwing down on its own reserves or is it a slowdown in China or what was the,
what was the reason for that? I don't think so. I think if you look at, I mean, it certainly helped
us, but I think it was more, it was self-interest. I mean, you look at the other Asian economies
that were, you know, Paris, Japan where they didn't have those kind of reserves. They had to have
massive demand destruction, which is going to impact your economy, trying to have the head,
you know, the headroom to do that as well. So I think it was, hey, we've been, you know,
this is what we have this for. Let's use it. I think I just think it was self-interest. Yeah,
that, you know, things are a little soft over there. The last thing they want is, you know,
significant impact from actions, external, you know, events outside their control.
And they know that, or they, at least the gamble is, you know, we use the reserves,
and then when the price goes back down, we refill them. The danger, of course, has been,
and remains, if this is not resolved, they get to the, and I think they're getting fairly close,
they're going to have to say, well, we have to start importing again. They also cancelled exports,
most of their exports too. So they were basically, you know, somewhat self-sufficient. And in a
tremendous amount of the Middle East oil goes to China. So they, they really said, all right,
you know, this is, this is what we have to do. So my opinion, yeah, I think it was self-interest.
I think it just happened to accommodate the U.S., but I could be wrong. There could have been
some coordination, but I kind of think it was more, what, what worked for China. I mean, Trump did
go visit Xi Jinping and Beijing, but it was a little bit, kind of like halfway through the,
halfway through the process. I just also wonder about the U.S. strategic petroleum reserve.
There's been this sort of increasing chatter that we're getting closer to the bottom. I don't
really know exactly what that means, whether that's like literally the, the, they drain it or whether
it sort of gets to a point where it's like not structurally sound if you pull too much, get oil out,
but it seemed to suggest that we're like weeks away from the bottom of the SPR. And I don't know
what the consequences of that, but do you, you can fill us in. What happens there?
Well, I think there's, well, first of all, I think, yes, you know, that, that is, that's out,
there are a number of other things where weeks away from this and that. I think this deal kind
of had to happen right now. And so, but yeah, with the SPR, it's at about 350 million barrels right
now. It is, is they've shown on the headlines. I mean, the last time it was that low was when they
were filling it back in 1983 and it first got to that level. It never, it got down to that level
in 22 when we had, you know, the, you know, we drained it for the Ukraine war really to keep prices
down, which they proved to be probably unnecessary. We didn't refill it for years. It started going back
up a little bit and we got over a camera with the exact number, but maybe four to 50 million barrels.
And then, you know, we've, we've dropped it again. So if you get down to about 300 million barrels,
that's the level where yes, it's functionally harder if not impossible to pump out. You do need
a certain level in the tank. So I mean, obviously, you can get it out, you know, if you absolutely
have to, but that might not be something where you can pump it as quickly as you could. So that
functional floor, we were getting close to despite the fact that 300 million barrels is, is a lot of
oil, but we really need to restock it. One thing about the, the oil that is getting restocked
right now or that will be restocked is the US government did something I think was fairly smart,
which is, you know, they sold swap contracts. So you can take a, you know, for every barrel,
you take, you have to give 1.25 barrels back. And so, you know, we'll be getting some additional,
you know, build back in, in the SPR, not just the barrels coming back, but even more beyond that.
So assuming we can get those barrels at a reasonable price, so that'll be the challenge.
Well, we saw, sorry, JC, I was going to say assuming that we do rebuild. I've been wondering if
everyone is not going to think like, well, I don't want to have this to happen again. Maybe I need
to be like 150% of what I was running out before it is to give myself more cushion. And maybe
like structurally, we just have higher oil prices for longer, don't study and play come together on

While supplies last and June 30, turns at aka.ms slash college PC.
Disney and Pixar's hoppers now available on Disney plus rated PG.
Yeah, I think that's very much a possibility. I don't know what happens in the US because
there's some dysfunction, but around the world, governments are talking about that. They're
talking about refilling and they are talking about that exact like 1.5 or more because yeah,
we can't handle this. It was a big wake up call in the US. You know, we're more cushioned from it,
so we can handle an SPR where it is right now, as long as we don't go much lower. But the rest
of the world, you know, can't and it's not just, not just oil, but it's, you know, distilled.
It's gasoline, it's jet fuel, so people are talking about top, you know, making reserves for those
where they might not have had them before as well. So, well, one thing I think the market may be missing
is that people are going to be, or countries are going to be building back up their reserves and
to, you know, higher levels and levels of reserves. And that will be probably put a floor on the
price for a while. Before we came into this year, oil was WTI was sub $60 and all of the valuations
that I had done, I'd pencil in like $80 oil was sort of a bullish outcome before it sort of
before the Iran conflict came around. Where do you see the oil prices now given that we may
need to do these refills across the board? There seems to have been some infrastructure destroyed
around the world. What do you think is a base case, bear case, bull case for oil over the next five
or 10 years? I think bear a five or 10 years, whatever time frame you film as comfortable with.
Yeah, I'll say for the next year, I mean, a lot of infrastructures damaged a lot. You know,
we have to clean up the straight-of-war moves. We have to get things flowing again. A lot of
barrels have drawn down, not just SPRs, but inventories around the globe. Commercial inventories have
been drawn down. So that all gets replenished. I think that over the next year, at least as that's
happening, you probably do have a floor. I'm always hesitant to, it's very difficult to put
those things. I mean, there's four millions of forecasts out there, but I think probably around 70,
I wouldn't be surprised by dips lower, something pessimistic happens, but I think I would probably
put an average, at least on Brentoyle, probably around 80, close to where it is now,
with spikes up into 90. It all depends again on global economy and all these external
factors that it's hard to foresee, but I'd kind of center it around 80. There's no guarantee
that's where it's going to be, but I definitely see it's a volatile commodity. So a dip down to 70,
at some point is possible. The one thing that could eventually push it down is OPEC is,
the one thing that supported the price for years was the OPEC put. Every time it got down to a
certain level, they were putting quotas in place and voluntarily restricting production, very much
a reverse of what they did in 2020. Now, even though they don't have the capability, they have
symbolically raised their output targets, and as they are able to fill those over the next year,
maybe not as high as they've published, but that's more oil going into the market. So
that'll be a little bit of a headwind of the price, but at the same time, a lot of structural
infrastructural damage and logistics that have to be prepared.
When you say that OPEC doesn't have the capability, what do you mean by that?
So they have, I think they're most recent, just last week, they are over the weekend, 188,
that were all the quotas and voluntary cuts they'd announced over the years,
several million barrels. They've been slowly, they started unwinding those in 2024
and putting a little oil back in the market very cautiously, and when the Iran were broke out,
they suddenly said, okay, we're going to throw 200, 400,000 barrels back into the market,
but they couldn't actually do that because straight-of-hormose was closed, a lot of oil was trapped,
and their production was shut in, because they can't get it out, they had to shut in production.
So they actually aren't producing that much. They have to restart facilities, they have to,
they have to get, they're well out through the street, there's this backlog, so even though they say,
all right, this is how much we'll produce. For example, Saudi Arabia, I think they're able to produce
about 7.5 million barrels a day right now, their quota, or they're in a level where they're
willing to go to, is at 10.25 million barrels a day. So they're basically, they're target,
they're 25% below their target, and every country in the Middle East is kind of in the same
border now. Let's talk natural gas. The, I understand that the U.S. is the Saudi Arabia
of natural gas. We got giant natural gas deposits, it's a strategic asset for the U.S.
Talk to us about the natural gas market, what do we need to know?
Yeah, I said, I mean, from 10 years ago when we exported no natural gas to now when we're
exporting 18, well, we're able, we're exporting about 16 billion cubic feet a day capacity right
now is 18.3. So that's a huge increase compared to where we were. There's a lot of terminals and
facilities that have come online that didn't exist 10 years ago, and there's another, I'm gonna say
four and a half billion cubic feet capacity coming online the next two, three years.
And it's really come online at a time when it's desperately needed. Europe needs our natural gas.
Yeah, we're shipping some days, but I think 68% of it goes to Europe with the outbreak of the
Ukraine war, and then the around war that's been desperately needed. But it's interesting because
U.S. natural gas, we have absolutely tons of it. It's a byproduct of crude oil production, which
of course is exploded with the shale revolution. We really don't have a problem with natural gas,
and that's kept prices low. It's always been a domestic market. It still is. It's going to be much
more affected by weather than exports. But that export story is becoming part of the story,
and over time, as we export more, and as it starts getting used in data centers, I think that'll
start, I mean, as the data centers are built out, as the AI story continues, which a lot of that
is powered by natural gas, there's a lot of demand there that is increasing. So where it's been,
I would say, almost a weather story for forever, it's becoming a demand story, both internally,
domestically and internationally with the exports. Australia actually is another big producer,
Qatar is a big producer, and that's the big thing going on is their big LNG facility was
severely damaged. They're trying to get back to about 50% capacity in a few months, 80% capacity
by the end of the year, which is pretty quick, but they're still going to be 20% of their capacity
that's going to be offline for years. And so that's probably about 4% of global LNG
supply. So that's going to be, that's something that's been taken out completely, and that is a
year's long story that's probably adding a bit of a premium to natural gas for the US, for Australia,
and everybody. It's long been sort of known as the, we don't make it trade, the gas trade,
because for whatever reason, it's extremely volatile. There seems like there's a lot of downward
pressure on prices most of the time, but every now and again, there's a massive spike, and that
takes people out. Is that how the market trades? How do you see the market?
Yeah, it's definitely a very volatile market for people that enjoy volatility, and we have some
of those fans of our funds. Natural gas certainly does that. The whole time that we've had our
natural gas fund out 19 years, UNG, not the whole time, but the majority of the time it has been
under pressure, and that is, again, is primarily this abundance, and then you get a cold weather
snap, or you get something that takes out production, and it does spike up. But I think that now
one thing that's, I don't know that the volatility goes away, but I do think that the price
is probably a bit inflationary. The channel is going to go up just because of that increased
demand, but it's definitely an exciting market, and I think we'll remain so long as we have
heat-cold and global disruption, so. Well, folks, we're coming up on the top of the hour. Let
me just give a shout out around the horn, and then JT's got some veggies for us.
Tombaltex is what's up, Tyler Valpareso. Toronto, London, UK.
Thanks for specifying, not Ontario, or London, Orlando, Florida, have a town.

you know, your breakthrough is coming and overnight success, 10 years in the making, right?
It's a nice story. It's also kind of wrong. And the real version tells us something about the

doing nothing for those five years. It's working the whole time. It's working underground,

and the machinery then is built that eventually allows it to raise the shoots. So here's

said it'll throw a small one or maybe no one at all. So it's time for us to now start torturing
